1: wayyĕṣaw ʾet-ʾăšer ʿal-bêtô lēʾmōr mallēʾ ʾet-ʾamtĕḥōt hāʾănāšîm ʾōḵel kaʾăšer yûḵlûn śēʾt — "Joseph commanded his household steward, 'Fill the men's sacks with food, as much as they can carry.'" The command is private, to the steward — the brothers have no knowledge of what is being arranged against them. The verb mallēʾ (fill, Piel of mālaʾ, H4390) — generous filling; Joseph is not setting a trap out of cruelty but designing an encounter that will bring the moral reckoning that the narrative requires for complete reconciliation.
2: wĕʾet-gaḇîʿî gaḇîaʿ hakkesep śîm bĕpî ʾamtaḥat haqqāṭān wĕʾēt kesef šiḇrô — "And put my cup — the silver cup — in the mouth of the youngest one's sack." Gaḇîaʿ (H1375, cup/goblet) — used only in this chapter (vv2,12,16,17) and in Jeremiah 35:5. The term denotes a specific type of wide-mouthed drinking cup, distinct from kôs (the cupbearer's serving cup in Genesis 40). The specificity "my cup, the silver cup" — the double-identification signals its value and personal significance; this is not just any cup but Joseph's personal vessel. Silver cups were among the most precious domestic objects in the ancient world.
3: habbōqer ʾôr wĕhāʾănāšîm šullĕḥû hēmmāh waḥămōrêhem — "At daybreak the men were sent off with their donkeys." Šullĕḥû (Pual of šālaḥ, H7971, to be sent away) — the passive: "they were sent away." The sending-verb that governs the Joseph narrative: in Genesis 45:5,7,8 Joseph will declare God sent him (šĕlāḥanî ʾĕlōhîm). Here the brothers are sent away — only to be brought back. The morning departure is routine; the overtaking will be the rupture.
4: hēm yāṣĕʾû ʾet-hāʿîr lōʾ hirḥîqû wĕyôsēp ʾāmar laʾăšer ʿal-bêtô qûm rĕḏōp ʾaḥărê hāʾănāšîm — "They had not gone far from the city when Joseph said to his steward, 'Pursue the men at once.'" The narrative compression — they had barely left, and Joseph immediately dispatches pursuit — creates tension. The steward becomes the instrument of accusation, not Joseph himself; this preserves Joseph's ability to receive them back in the house and eventually unmask himself privately (Genesis 45:1).
5: hălôʾ zeh ʾăšer yište ʾăḏōnî bô wĕhûʾ naḥēš yĕnaḥēš bô — "Is this not the cup my lord drinks from and uses for divination (naḥēš yĕnaḥēš)?" Naḥēš yĕnaḥēš (Piel infinitive absolute + imperfect of nāḥaš, H5172, to practice divination/augury) — the emphatic construction: "he divines with divining." Egyptian hydromancy — reading omens from liquid poured into a silver or bronze cup — was a documented practice. Whether Joseph actually practiced this is ambiguous; the steward may be embellishing the accusation for effect. Genesis never presents Joseph as a diviner by practice; his interpretations are consistently attributed to God (Genesis 40:8; Genesis 41:16). The claim functions as legal intensification: the stolen item is not just valuable but sacred.
6: wayyassiguhem wayyĕḏabbēr ʾălêhem ʾet-haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh — "He overtook them and spoke these words to them." Wayyassiguhem (Hiphil of nāśag, H5381, to overtake/catch up) — the pursuit-catch verb; the same word used for Laban overtaking Jacob (Genesis 31:23,25). The overtaking is swift; the accusation immediately follows without opportunity for defense.
7: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾēlāyw lāmmāh yĕḏabbēr ʾăḏōnî kaddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh ḥālîlāh laʿăḇāḏeḵā mēʿăśôṯ kaddāḇār hazzeh — "They said to him, 'Why does my lord speak such words as these? Far be it (ḥālîlāh) from your servants to do such a thing!'" Ḥālîlāh (H2486, far be it / God forbid / it is a desecration) — the strongest oath-formula in biblical Hebrew, invoking the idea that the suggested act would be a ḥālāl (a desecration, a profaning of something sacred). Used by Abraham ("Far be it from you to do such a thing!" Genesis 18:25), by Joseph himself (Genesis 44:17), by David ("Far be it from me, YHWH!" 1 Samuel 26:11). The brothers' protest is forceful — this is not a mild denial but an oath-formula.
8: hēn kesef ʾăšer māṣāʾnû bĕpî ʾamtĕḥōtênû hăšîḇōnû ʾēleḵā mēʾereṣ kĕnaʿan wĕʾêḵāh ninōb mibêt ʾăḏōneḵā kesef ʾô zāhāḇ — "We even returned to you the silver we found in our sacks; how then could we steal silver or gold from your master's house?" The argument is logical: men who returned found silver voluntarily would not steal from the master's own house. The brothers use the evidence of their own honesty (the returned silver of Genesis 42) as the exculpatory argument. Ironically, the returned silver was itself Joseph's arrangement — he is now using their honesty to test them further.
9: ʾăšer yimmāṣēʾ ʾittô mēʿăḇāḏeḵā wāmēt wĕgam-ʾănaḥnû nihyeh laʾăḏōnî laʿăḇāḏîm — "Whichever of your servants has it shall die, and the rest of us will become my lord's slaves." The self-condemning offer is legally reckless — they are so confident of their innocence that they offer the maximum penalty. The word wāmēt (and he shall die) echoes the earlier "we will surely die" if they go to Egypt without Benjamin (Genesis 42:20). The brothers have moved from fearing death to offering it as proof of innocence. When the cup is found in Benjamin's sack, this oath will hang over them.
10: wayyōʾmer gam-ʿattāh ḵiḏḇārêḵem kēn-hûʾ ʾăšer yimmāṣēʾ ʾittô yihyeh-llî ʿāḇeḏ wĕʾattem tihyû nĕqiyyîm — "He said, 'Very well; let it be as you say — whoever is found with it will be my slave, but the rest of you will be free from blame.'" Nĕqiyyîm (H5355, innocent/free from guilt) — the steward modifies the brothers' oath: instead of death + slavery for all, only the offender becomes a slave; the rest go free. This modification is crucial: it creates the scenario in Genesis 45 where Judah must choose between keeping his pledge to his father (bring Benjamin back) and the steward's offer (go home free). The test is designed to reveal whether the brothers will again abandon a son of Rachel to save themselves.
11: wayyĕmahărû wayyôrîdû ʾîš ʾet-ʾamtaḥtô ʾarṣāh wayyipĕtĕḥû ʾîš ʾamtaḥtô — "Each man quickly lowered his sack and opened it." Wayyĕmahărû (Piel of māhar, H4116, to hurry) — the urgency of the confident: they hurry to prove their innocence. The speed of compliance contrasts with what is about to happen; their hurry only accelerates their devastation.
12: wayyĕḥappēś baggāḏôl hēḥēl ûḇaqqāṭōn killāh wayyimmāṣēʾ haggāḇîaʿ bĕʾamtaḥat binyāmîn — "He searched, beginning with the oldest and finishing with the youngest; the cup was found in Benjamin's sack." The search begins with the eldest — adding suspense by confirming each brother's innocence one by one — and ends with Benjamin. The word wayyimmāṣēʾ (and it was found, Niphal of māṣāʾ, H4672) — the passive "was found"; this is the same verb used for Joseph's cup being "found" and for Judah's confession that "God has found out our guilt" (Genesis 44:16). The finding-verb carries weight throughout the chapter.
13: wayyiqrĕʾû śimlōtām wayyaʿămas ʾîš ʿal-ḥămōrô wayyāšûḇû hāʿîrāh — "They tore their garments (wayyiqrĕʾû śimlōtām), each loaded his donkey, and returned to the city." Qāraʿ śĕmālāh (to tear a garment, H7167 + H8071) — the formal mourning-gesture for catastrophic loss or profound grief; Jacob tore his garments when he believed Joseph was dead (Genesis 37:34). The brothers' tearing of garments signals that they experience this as a death — the death of their return, of Benjamin's freedom, of their father's remaining hope. They all return — not just Benjamin — which is the first evidence that the brothers have changed.
14: wayyāḇōʾ yĕhûḏāh wĕʾeḥāyw bêtāh yôsēp wĕhûʾ ʿôḏennû šām wayyippĕlû lĕpānāyw ʾarṣāh — "Judah and his brothers came to Joseph's house; Joseph was still there. They fell to the ground before him." Wayyippĕlû lĕpānāyw ʾarṣāh (they fell before him to the ground) — a prostration more abject than the earlier bowing: nāpal (H5307, to fall) rather than hishtaḥăwāh (to bow/prostrate). The complete fulfillment of Joseph's second dream (Genesis 37:9-10: "the sun and moon and eleven stars bowed to me") — all eleven brothers prostrate before him. Judah is named first as the spokesman who will carry the brothers' fate.
15: wayyōʾmer lāhem yôsēp māh-hammǎʿăśeh hazzeh ʾăšer ʿăśîtem hălôʾ yĕḏaʿtem kî-naḥēš yĕnaḥēš ʾîš ʾăšer kāmōnî — "Joseph said to them, 'What is this deed that you have done? Do you not know that a man like me can divine?'" Naḥēš yĕnaḥēš (Piel infinitive absolute + imperfect) again — the emphasis on Joseph's supposed divinatory power. The question is rhetorical; Joseph knows exactly what happened. His claim to special knowledge sets up the brothers' theological interpretation in v16: "God has found out our guilt." They take the accusation as divine disclosure.
16: wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh mah-nōmar laʾăḏōnî mah-nĕḏabbēr ûmah-niṣṭaddāq hāʾĕlōhîm māṣāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōn ʿăḇāḏeḵā — "Judah said, 'What can we say to my lord? What can we speak? How can we justify ourselves (niṣṭaddāq)? God has found out the guilt of your servants.'" Three rhetorical questions in sequence — mah-nōmar… mah-nĕḏabbēr… ûmah-niṣṭaddāq — produce a speechless conclusion: there is nothing to say, nothing to speak, no self-justification possible. Niṣṭaddāq (Hithpael of ṣāḏaq, H6663, to justify oneself) — echoes Tamar's vindication in Genesis 38:26 (ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî). Hāʾĕlōhîm māṣāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōn (God has found out the guilt) — Judah interprets the cup-finding as divine disclosure of their real guilt — the selling of Joseph. They are not confessing to the cup-theft (they didn't do it) but to the unresolved, unconfessed evil of Genesis 37. God's providence has brought them to the moment of reckoning.
17: wayyōʾmer ḥālîlāh llî mēʿăśôṯ zōʾt hāʾîš ʾăšer nimmāṣāʾ haggāḇîaʿ bĕyāḏô hûʾ yihyeh-llî ʿāḇeḏ wĕʾattem ʿălû lĕšālôm ʾel-ʾăḇîḵem — "He said, 'Far be it (ḥālîlāh) from me to do such a thing! Only the man in whose hand the cup was found shall be my slave; as for you, go up in peace to your father.'" Joseph uses the ḥālîlāh oath-formula himself — matching the brothers' oath of v7. His offer is the same as the steward's (v10): only the offender stays, the rest go free. This is the pivot point of the test: will the brothers walk away from Benjamin as they walked away from Joseph?
18: wayyiggaš ʾēlāyw yĕhûḏāh wayyōʾmer bî ʾăḏōnî yĕḏabbēr-nāʾ ʿaḇdĕḵā ḏāḇār bĕʾoznê ʾăḏōnî wĕʾal-yiḥar ʾappĕḵā bĕʿaḇdĕḵā — "Then Judah came near (wayyiggaš) to him and said, 'Please, my lord, let your servant speak a word in my lord's ears, and let not your anger burn against your servant.'" Wayyiggaš (Niphal of nāgaš, H5066, to draw near/approach) — the approach of the intercessor. This verb is used for the approach of prayer, petition, and covenant negotiation: Abraham's drawing near to plead for Sodom (Genesis 18:23: wayyiggaš ʾaḇrāhām), Moses drawing near to speak with God (Exodus 24:2). Judah's approach to Joseph mirrors Abraham's approach to YHWH — a mortal approaching ultimate authority to plead for others. The plea that follows (vv18-34) is the most sustained piece of rhetoric in Genesis.
19: ʾăḏōnî šāʾal ʾet-ʿăḇāḏāyw lēʾmōr ha-yēš lāḵem ʾāḇ ʾô-ʾāḥ — "My lord asked his servants, saying, 'Have you a father or a brother?'" Judah begins his speech with a recounting of events — the classic structure of a legal defense: state the facts chronologically. By beginning with Joseph's own question, Judah establishes that the family details were disclosed in response to interrogation, not volunteered. The speech covers five sections: (1) the disclosure of Benjamin's existence (vv19-21); (2) Joseph's demand for Benjamin (vv22-23); (3) Jacob's resistance (vv24-29); (4) the stakes — Jacob's life (vv30-31); (5) Judah's substitution offer (vv32-34).
20: wayyōmer yēš-lānû ʾāḇ zāqēn wĕyeled zĕqunîm qāṭān wĕʾāḥîw mēt wayyiwwāṯer hûʾ lĕḇaddô lĕʾimmô wĕʾāḇîw ʾăhēḇô — "We said to my lord, 'We have an aged father, and a young child of his old age; his brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loves him.'" Zĕqunîm (old age's child, H2207 — child born to an old man, with the emotional investment that implies) — same term for Joseph in Genesis 37:3: "ben-zĕqunîm." Benjamin occupies the same position Joseph had — the beloved son of Jacob's old age, the surviving son of Rachel. Judah's recounting for Joseph that "his brother is dead" — telling Joseph that he himself is "dead" — is the narrative's deepest irony. Lĕḇaddô (alone, H905) — the solitude of Jacob without Benjamin echoes Jacob's solitude after Joseph's "death."
21: waṯṯōʾmer ʾel-ʿăḇāḏeḵā hôrîḏuhû ʾēlay wĕʾāśîmāh ʿênî ʿālāyw — "You said to your servants, 'Bring him down to me, so I may set my eyes on him.'" Wĕʾāśîmāh ʿênî ʿālāyw (I will set my eye on him) — the idiom for special personal attention and protective oversight. Judah quotes Joseph's own words back to him: Joseph's protective gaze toward Benjamin is now the basis of the plea. The "setting of eyes" is also the verb of special attention used for divine oversight of the land of Canaan (Deuteronomy 11:12: "YHWH's eyes are always on it").
22: wayyōʾmer ʾel-ʿăḇāḏeḵā lōʾ-yûḵal hannáʿar laʿăzōḇ ʾet-ʾāḇîw wĕʿāzaḇ ʾet-ʾāḇîw wāmēt — "We said to my lord, 'The boy cannot leave his father; if he were to leave his father, his father would die.'" The causal chain is explicitly stated: Benjamin's absence → father's death. This is not rhetorical embellishment — Jacob had already nearly been destroyed by Joseph's "death" (Genesis 37:35: "he refused to be comforted"). Judah is explaining a clinical family fact. The word wāmēt (and he would die) — death as the consequence of separation, echoing the life/death polarity of the whole narrative.
23: waṯṯōʾmer ʾel-ʿăḇāḏeḵā ʾim-lōʾ yērēḏ ʾăḥîḵem haqqāṭōn ʾittĕḵem lōʾ tōsipûn lirʾôt pānāy — "You said to your servants, 'If your youngest brother does not come down with you, you will not see my face again.'" Judah quotes Joseph's ultimatum back to him — not as accusation but as explanation of the bind they are in. The phrase lirʾôt pānāy (to see my face) echoes the original warning in Genesis 43:3 (hēʿēḏ hēʿîḏ ḇānû hāʾîš: "the man solemnly warned us"). Judah is showing Joseph that every step they took was in response to Joseph's own commands.
24: wayhî kî ʿālînû ʾel-ʿaḇdĕḵā ʾāḇî wannaggēd-lô ʾēt diḇrê ʾăḏōnî — "When we went up to your servant my father, we told him what my lord had said." The report to Jacob is faithful: the brothers transmitted Joseph's conditions accurately. Judah's repetition of the events is establishing their honesty throughout — they have been obedient to the official's commands at every step.
25: wayyōʾmer ʾāḇînû šûḇû šiḇrû-lānû mĕʿaṭ-ʾōḵel — "Our father said, 'Go back and buy a little food.'" Jacob's minimizing language — mĕʿaṭ (a little) — reflects his reluctance even in need; he cannot bring himself to send Benjamin and tries to limit the exposure by limiting the trip.
26: wayyōʾmer lōʾ nûḵal lārēḏet ʾim-yeš-ʾāḥînû haqqāṭōn ʾittānû wĕyāraḏnû — "We said, 'We cannot go down; only if our youngest brother is with us will we go down.'" The brothers' condition is absolute: no Benjamin, no Egypt. This resolves Jacob's wavering with a hard constraint.
27: wayyōʾmer ʿaḇdĕḵā ʾāḇî ʾēlênû ʾattem yĕḏaʿtem kî šĕnayim yālĕḏāh-llî ʾišti — "Your servant my father said to us, 'You know that my wife bore me two sons.'" Judah quotes Jacob's speech — and Jacob's words are devastating in their dramatic irony: "my wife" — Rachel, the beloved wife — "bore me two sons." Jacob has two surviving sons of Rachel: Joseph (who he believes is dead) and Benjamin. Joseph is hearing his own father speak of his death through Judah's mouth.
28: wayyēṣēʾ hāʾeḥāḏ mēʾittî wāʾōmar ʾak ṭārōp ṭōrap wayyĕhî-llô-rōph ʿaḏ-hēnnāh — "The one went out from me, and I said, 'Surely he has been torn apart (ṭārōp ṭōrap)'; and I have not seen him since." Ṭārōp ṭōrap (Qal infinitive absolute + perfect of ṭārap, H2963, to tear/rend — as a wild animal tears prey) — the same construction the brothers used when they deceived Jacob with Joseph's coat: "this we found — hakkēr-nāʾ — is it your son's?" and Jacob concluded "a wild animal has torn him" (Genesis 37:33: ṭārōp ṭōrap yôsēp). Judah is quoting Jacob quoting the brothers' lie back to Joseph — the innocent man hears his own murder being described by the man who served as its architect. The dramatic irony is almost unbearable.
29: ûlĕqaḥtem gam-ʾet-zeh mēʿim pānay wĕqārāhû ʾāsôn wĕhôraḏtem ʾet-śêḇātî bĕrāʿāh šĕʾōlāh — "If you take this one from me also and harm comes to him, you will bring my gray hairs down to Sheol in grief." Śêḇāh (H7872, gray hair/old age — the physical sign of accumulated years) + šĕʾōl (H7585, the underworld/grave/place of the dead) — "you will bring my gray hair to Sheol." Jacob's life is at stake in Benjamin's safety. This is not mere rhetoric; Judah is conveying his father's literal condition. The phrase completes the arc of Jacob's suffering: the gray-haired patriarch who has already lost one beloved son cannot survive losing the other.
30: wĕʿattāh kĕḇōʾî ʾel-ʿaḇdĕḵā ʾāḇî wĕhannáʿar ʾênennu ʾittānû wĕnapšô qĕšûrāh ḇĕnapšô — "Now if I come to your servant my father and the boy is not with us — since his life (napšô) is bound up in the boy's life (bĕnapšô) —" Napšô qĕšûrāh ḇĕnapšô (his life is bound up in his life) — nāpāš (H5315, soul/life/self/person) twice, with qāšar (H7194, to bind/tie together) between them. The binding-together of two souls is the most intimate form of covenant-love in biblical Hebrew: Jonathan's soul "bound" to David's soul (1 Samuel 18:1: nĕpešô niqšĕrāh bĕnepěš dāwîḏ). Jacob's soul is bound to Benjamin's as covenant-love binds two persons. If Benjamin dies, Jacob's soul tears free — which is death.
31: wĕhāyāh kirʾōtô kî-ʾên hannáʿar wāmēt — "When he sees that the boy is not with us, he will die." The stark prediction — four Hebrew words — is Judah's climactic statement of the stakes. No rhetorical elaboration; just the blunt fact: no Benjamin → father dies. This is the weight against which Judah will offer himself.
32: kî ʿaḇdĕḵā ʿāraḇ ʾet-hannáʿar mēʿim ʾāḇî lēʾmōr ʾim-lōʾ ʾăḇîʾennû ʾēleḵā wĕḥāṭāʾtî lĕʾāḇî kāl-hayyāmîm — "For your servant became surety (ʿāraḇ) for the boy to my father, saying, 'If I do not bring him back to you, I will bear the blame before my father all my days.'" ʿĀraḇ (H6148, to become surety/pledge, Qal perfect) — Judah's surety-pledge from 43:9 is now his binding obligation. The same root as ʿēreḇôn (pledge, Genesis 38) — but there it was a careless pledge; here it is a life-pledge. Judah's transformation is complete: the man who sold Joseph for twenty pieces of silver now pledges his own life for Benjamin.
33: wĕʿattāh yēšeḇ-nāʾ ʿaḇdĕḵā taḥat hannáʿar ʿeḇeḏ laʾăḏōnî wĕhannáʿar yaʿal ʿim-ʾeḥāyw — "Now let your servant remain instead of (taḥat) the boy as a slave to my lord, and let the boy go up with his brothers." Taḥat (H8478, under/in place of/instead of) — the substitution preposition. This is the same word used for Abraham's ram offered in Isaac's place: wayyaʿălēhû lĕʿōlāh taḥat bĕnô (he offered it as a burnt offering in place of his son, Genesis 22:13). Judah's voluntary substitution — "I instead of the boy" — is structurally identical to the ram's substitution for Isaac. The man who failed to substitute himself for Joseph in Genesis 37:26-27 (suggesting instead they sell him) now offers himself as a substitutionary slave. The moral reversal is complete and the echo to the Akedah is intentional.
34: kî-ʾêḵāh ʾeʿĕleh ʾel-ʾāḇî wĕhannáʿar ʾênennu ʾittî pen ʾerʾeh bārāʿ ʾăšer yimṣāʾ ʾet-ʾāḇî — "For how can I go back to my father if the boy is not with me? I am afraid of the evil that would come upon my father." ʾêḵāh ʾeʿĕleh (how can I go up?) — the rhetorical question as final argument. Bārāʿ ʾăšer yimṣāʾ ʾet-ʾāḇî (the evil/harm that would find my father) — māṣāʾ (to find/befall) used for evil overtaking someone; the same verb that found the cup (v12) and that God used to find their guilt (v16). Judah's speech ends not with self-justification but with his father's welfare. He has moved entirely outside himself.
